title: Fixing Invalid Height Calculation in a Binary Tree

statement.markdown: Given a binary tree where each node has a value and two children (left and right), calculate the height of the tree. The height of a tree is the number of nodes along the longest path from the root node down to the farthest leaf node. The height of an empty tree is 0. Implement a function that calculates the height of the binary tree.
statement.inputFormat: Each test case will contain a binary tree represented as an array of integers where for each node at index `i` the children are at index `i * 2 + 1` and `i * 2 + 2`.
statement.outputFormat: The height of the binary tree as an integer.
statement.notes: Note: This is a bug-fix challenge. Your task is to correct a given buggy code that calculates the height of the binary tree.

examples.explanation: Height of the tree is 3 as the longest path from root to leaf is 1 -> 2 -> 5 -> 6.
Height of the tree is 2 as the longest path from root to leaf is 1 -> 2 -> 3.

hints.text: Use depth-first search to calculate the height of the tree.

editorial.title: Editorial Solution
editorial.intuition: We need to write a function that calculates the height of a binary tree using depth-first search.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute-force approach can be implemented using a recursive function that calculates the height of the left and right subtrees and returns the maximum of the two plus one.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution can be implemented using a recursive function that calculates the height of the left and right subtrees and returns the maximum of the two plus one.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the solution can be proved by analyzing the recursive calls and the base cases.
editorial.dryRun: A dry run of the solution can be performed by calculating the height of a small binary tree and checking the results.
commonMistakes: Not handling the base case of an empty tree
Not calculating the height of the left and right subtrees

starterCode.java: class Solution {public int height(int[] root) { // TODO: implement } }
starterCode.python: class Solution: def height(self, root: list[int]) -> int: # TODO: implement

buggyStarterCode.cpp: class Solution {public: int height(vector<int>& root) { if (root.size() == 0) return 1; return 1 + height(root[1]); } };
buggyStarterCode.java: class Solution {public int height(int[] root) { if (root.length == 0) return 1; return 1 + height(root[1]); } }
buggyStarterCode.python: class Solution: def height(self, root: list[int]) -> int: if len(root) == 0: return 1; return 1 + self.height(root[1])
buggyStarterCode.javascript: function height(root) { if (root.length == 0) return 1; return 1 + height(root[1]); }

solutions.cpp: class Solution {public: int height(vector<int>& root) { if (root.size() == 0) return 0; int left = height(root[2 * i + 1]); int right = height(root[2 * i + 2]); return (left > right ? left : right) + 1; } };
solutions.python: class Solution: def height(self, root: list[int]) -> int: if len(root) == 0: return 0; n = len(root); left = self.height(root[2 * n:]) if left else 0; right = self.height(root[2 * n + 1:]) if len(root[2 * n + 1:]) else 0; return max(left, right) + 1
